# Compute log-likelihood matrix
S = length(α_samples)
log_lik_matrix = zeros(n, S)

for i in 1:n, s in 1:S
    μ_pred = α_samples[s] + dot(X[i, :], β_samples[s, :])
    log_lik_matrix[i, s] = logpdf(Normal(μ_pred, σ_samples[s]), y[i])
end

# Apply PSIS-LOO
total_loo, loo_est, k_vals = psis_loo(log_lik_matrix)

println("Total PSIS-LOO: $(round(total_loo, digits=2))")
println("Max k value: $(round(maximum(k_vals), digits=3))")
println("Problematic observations (k > 0.7): $(sum(k_vals .> 0.7))")

4.11 Repeated Cross-Validation

Single cross-validation runs suffer from dependence on the particular data partition. Repeated cross-validation addresses this limitation by performing multiple CV runs with different random splits, then averaging results.

For \(R\) repetitions of \(K\)-fold cross-validation:

\[\text{RCV}_{K,R} = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=1}^R \text{CV}_{K,r}\]

This approach reduces the variance of the CV estimate at the cost of increased computation. The variance reduction follows from the law of large numbers, though the improvement diminishes as correlation between repetitions increases.

Theorem 7.7 (Repeated CV Variance). Under independence of repetitions, \[\text{Var}[\text{RCV}_{K,R}] = \frac{1}{R} \text{Var}[\text{CV}_K]\]

function repeated_kfold_cv(X, y, model_fit_fn, predict_fn, loss_fn; 
                          K=10, R=10, seeds=nothing)
    if seeds === nothing
        seeds = rand(1:10000, R)
    end
    
    cv_scores = Float64[]
    
    for r in 1:R
        score, _, _ = kfold_cv(X, y, model_fit_fn, predict_fn, loss_fn; 
                              K=K, seed=seeds[r])
        push!(cv_scores, score)
    end
    
    return mean(cv_scores), std(cv_scores), cv_scores
end

# Example: Compare single vs repeated CV stability
Random.seed!(202)
n = 100
X = randn(n, 5)
y = X * [1, -1, 0.5, 0, -0.3] + 0.2 * randn(n)

# Simple linear regression functions
linear_fit(X, y) = X \ y
linear_predict(β, X) = X * β
squared_loss(y_true, y_pred) = (y_true - y_pred)^2

# Single run variability
single_runs = [kfold_cv(X, y, linear_fit, linear_predict, squared_loss, K=10)[1] 
               for _ in 1:20]
single_std = std(single_runs)

# Repeated CV with different R values
R_values = [1, 5, 10, 20]
repeated_stds = Float64[]

for R in R_values
    repeated_scores = [repeated_kfold_cv(X, y, linear_fit, linear_predict, squared_loss, 
                                       K=10, R=R)[1] for _ in 1:20]
    push!(repeated_stds, std(repeated_scores))
end

println("Single CV standard deviation: $(round(single_std, digits=4))")
for (R, std_val) in zip(R_values, repeated_stds)
    println("R=$R CV standard deviation: $(round(std_val, digits=4))")
end

4.12 Nested Cross-Validation

When models involve hyperparameters requiring tuning, standard cross-validation provides biased performance estimates. Nested cross-validation addresses this by using separate CV loops for hyperparameter selection and performance assessment.

The outer loop partitions data for unbiased performance estimation, while the inner loop selects optimal hyperparameters using only the outer training set. This ensures that test data never influences hyperparameter choices, maintaining the integrity of performance estimates.

Algorithm 7.2 (Nested Cross-Validation). 1. Outer loop: Split data into \(K_{\text{outer}}\) folds 2. For each outer fold \(k\): - Set aside fold \(k\) as test set - Use remaining data as outer training set - Inner loop: Apply \(K_{\text{inner}}\)-fold CV to outer training set - Select hyperparameters minimizing inner CV error - Fit model with selected hyperparameters on outer training set - Evaluate on outer test set 3. Average outer test errors for final performance estimate

function nested_cv(X, y, hyperparams, model_fit_fn, predict_fn, loss_fn; 
                   K_outer=5, K_inner=5, seed=123)
    Random.seed!(seed)
    n = length(y)
    
    # Outer fold assignments
    outer_folds = [Int[] for _ in 1:K_outer]
    indices = shuffle(1:n)
    fold_size = n ÷ K_outer
    
    for i in 1:n
        fold_idx = min(ceil(Int, i / fold_size), K_outer)
        push!(outer_folds[fold_idx], indices[i])
    end
    
    outer_errors = Float64[]
    selected_hyperparams = []
    
    for k in 1:K_outer
        println("Outer fold $k/$K_outer")
        
        # Split into outer train/test
        outer_test_idx = outer_folds[k]
        outer_train_idx = setdiff(1:n, outer_test_idx)
        
        X_outer_train = X[outer_train_idx, :]
        y_outer_train = y[outer_train_idx]
        X_outer_test = X[outer_test_idx, :]
        y_outer_test = y[outer_test_idx]
        
        # Inner cross-validation for hyperparameter selection
        best_hyperparam = nothing
        best_inner_score = Inf
        
        for hyperparam in hyperparams
            # Inner CV score
            inner_score, _, _ = kfold_cv(
                X_outer_train, y_outer_train,
                (X, y) -> model_fit_fn(X, y, hyperparam),
                predict_fn, loss_fn;
                K=K_inner, seed=seed + k
            )
            
            if inner_score < best_inner_score
                best_inner_score = inner_score
                best_hyperparam = hyperparam
            end
        end
        
        push!(selected_hyperparams, best_hyperparam)
        
        # Fit with best hyperparameter on outer training set
        final_model = model_fit_fn(X_outer_train, y_outer_train, best_hyperparam)
        
        # Evaluate on outer test set
        outer_pred = predict_fn(final_model, X_outer_test)
        outer_error = mean([loss_fn(y_outer_test[i], outer_pred[i]) 
                           for i in 1:length(y_outer_test)])
        push!(outer_errors, outer_error)
        
        println("  Selected hyperparam: $best_hyperparam, Error: $(round(outer_error, digits=4))")
    end
    
    return mean(outer_errors), std(outer_errors), outer_errors, selected_hyperparams
end

# Example: Ridge regression with nested CV
function ridge_fit_hp(X, y, λ)
    X_aug = hcat(ones(size(X, 1)), X)
    n, p = size(X_aug)
    
    # Analytical solution for ridge regression
    β = inv(X_aug' * X_aug + λ * [0 0; 0 I(p-1)]) * X_aug' * y
    return β
end

ridge_predict(β, X) = hcat(ones(size(X, 1)), X) * β

# Generate data with optimal λ around 0.1
Random.seed!(303)
n, p = 80, 10
X = randn(n, p)
y = X * randn(p) + 0.5 * randn(n)

# Hyperparameter grid
λ_grid = 10.0.^(-3:0.5:1)

# Standard CV (biased)
standard_cv_scores = Float64[]
for λ in λ_grid
    cv_score, _, _ = kfold_cv(X, y, 
        (X, y) -> ridge_fit_hp(X, y, λ),
        ridge_predict, squared_loss, K=10)
    push!(standard_cv_scores, cv_score)
end
best_λ_standard = λ_grid[argmin(standard_cv_scores)]

# Nested CV (unbiased)
nested_score, nested_std, _, selected_λs = nested_cv(
    X, y, λ_grid, ridge_fit_hp, ridge_predict, squared_loss,
    K_outer=5, K_inner=5
)

println("Standard CV best λ: $(round(best_λ_standard, digits=4))")
println("Standard


\newpage

# Chapter 8: Optimization Algorithms for Statistics

Statistical inference frequently reduces to optimization problems. Maximum likelihood estimation requires maximizing the log-likelihood function. Bayesian MAP estimation seeks the mode of the posterior distribution. Regularized methods like LASSO minimize penalized objective functions. This chapter develops the optimization algorithms that make modern computational statistics possible, from classical Newton-Raphson to modern stochastic gradient methods and their applications across statistical models.

## Classical Methods for Statistical Optimization

### Newton-Raphson and Fisher Scoring

The Newton-Raphson method forms the foundation of statistical optimization. Given a twice-differentiable objective function f(θ), the method uses the local quadratic approximation to find the optimum.

> **Theorem 8.1 (Newton-Raphson Update).** For a twice-differentiable function f: ℝᵈ → ℝ, the Newton-Raphson iteration is
> 
> θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ = θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - H⁻¹(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)
> 
> where H(θ) = ∇²f(θ) is the Hessian matrix.

*Proof.* The second-order Taylor expansion around θ⁽ᵗ⁾ gives
f(θ) ≈ f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) + ∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)ᵀ(θ - θ⁽ᵗ⁾) + ½(θ - θ⁽ᵗ⁾)ᵀH(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)(θ - θ⁽ᵗ⁾).
Setting the gradient of this approximation to zero and solving for θ yields the update rule. □

Fisher scoring modifies Newton-Raphson for maximum likelihood estimation by replacing the observed Hessian with the expected Fisher information matrix.

> **Definition 8.1 (Fisher Information Matrix).** For a parametric model with log-likelihood ℓ(θ), the Fisher information matrix is
> 
> I(θ) = -E[∇²ℓ(θ)] = E[∇ℓ(θ)∇ℓ(θ)ᵀ]
> 
> where the expectation is taken with respect to the true distribution.

Fisher scoring replaces the Hessian H(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) = ∇²ℓ(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) with I(θ⁽ᵗ⁾), yielding:

θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ = θ⁽ᵗ⁾ + I⁻¹(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)∇ℓ(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)

This modification has several advantages: the Fisher information is always positive definite for identifiable models, ensuring ascent direction; it connects directly to asymptotic variance; and it often provides more stable convergence than pure Newton-Raphson.

**Example 8.1: Logistic Regression via Fisher Scoring**

Consider logistic regression with log-likelihood ℓ(β) = Σᵢ[yᵢxᵢᵀβ - log(1 + exp(xᵢᵀβ))]. The gradient and Fisher information are:

∇ℓ(β) = Xᵀ(y - p), I(β) = XᵀWX

where p = (1 + exp(-Xβ))⁻¹ and W = diag(pᵢ(1-pᵢ)).

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, Optimization

function logistic_fisher_scoring(X, y; maxiter=100, tol=1e-6)
    n, p = size(X)
    β = zeros(p)  # Initialize parameters
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # Compute linear predictor and probabilities
        η = X * β
        μ = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-η))
        
        # Gradient (score function)
        score = X' * (y - μ)
        
        # Fisher information matrix
        W = diagm(μ .* (1 .- μ))
        I_fisher = X' * W * X
        
        # Check for convergence
        if norm(score) < tol
            return β, iter
        end
        
        # Fisher scoring update
        β += I_fisher \ score
    end
    
    return β, maxiter
end

# Example: Simulate logistic regression data
using Random
Random.seed!(123)

n, p = 1000, 3
X = [ones(n) randn(n, p-1)]
β_true = [0.5, 1.2, -0.8]
η = X * β_true
y = rand.(Bernoulli.(1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-η))))

# Fit using Fisher scoring
β_hat, iterations = logistic_fisher_scoring(X, y)
println("True β: ", β_true)
println("Estimated β: ", β_hat)
println("Converged in $iterations iterations")

4.12.1 Convergence Theory

The convergence properties of Newton-Raphson depend critically on the problem structure and initialization.

Theorem 8.2 (Local Quadratic Convergence). Suppose f is twice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of θ, ∇f(θ) = 0, and H(θ) is positive definite. Then there exists δ > 0 such that if ||θ⁽⁰⁾ - θ|| < δ, the Newton-Raphson sequence converges quadratically to θ*.

Proof. The error eᵗ = θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - θ* satisfies eᵗ⁺¹ = -H⁻¹(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)[∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) - H(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)eᵗ]. By Taylor expansion and continuity of H⁻¹, ||eᵗ⁺¹|| ≤ C||eᵗ||² for some constant C when θ⁽ᵗ⁾ is sufficiently close to θ*. □

This quadratic convergence is remarkable but requires good initialization. For statistical problems, method-of-moments estimators or other consistent initial estimators often provide suitable starting points.

4.13 Quasi-Newton Methods

Computing and inverting the Hessian matrix at each iteration can be computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional problems. Quasi-Newton methods approximate the Hessian using gradient information accumulated across iterations.

4.13.1 BFGS Algorithm

The Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) algorithm maintains an approximation Bₜ to the inverse Hessian H⁻¹(θ⁽ᵗ⁾).

Algorithm 8.1 (BFGS Method). Given initial θ⁽⁰⁾ and positive definite B₀: 1. Compute search direction: d⁽ᵗ⁾ = -Bₜ∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) 2. Line search: θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ = θ⁽ᵗ⁾ + αₜd⁽ᵗ⁾ 3. Update approximation: - sₜ = θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ - θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - yₜ = ∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾) - ∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾) - Bₜ₊₁ = Bₜ - (BₜyₜyₜᵀBₜ)/(yₜᵀBₜyₜ) + (sₜsₜᵀ)/(yₜᵀsₜ)

The BFGS update satisfies the secant condition Bₜ₊₁yₜ = sₜ and maintains positive definiteness under mild conditions.

4.13.2 Limited-Memory BFGS (L-BFGS)

For high-dimensional problems, storing the full matrix Bₜ becomes prohibitive. L-BFGS stores only the last m pairs (sᵢ, yᵢ) and implicitly computes the BFGS approximation.

using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

function rosenbrock(x, p)
    return (1 - x[1])^2 + 100 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2
end

function rosenbrock_grad!(g, x, p)
    g[1] = -2 * (1 - x[1]) - 400 * x[1] * (x[2] - x[1]^2)
    g[2] = 200 * (x[2] - x[1]^2)
    return g
end

# Comparison of optimization methods
x0 = [-1.2, 1.0]

# Newton-Raphson with exact Hessian
function rosenbrock_hess!(H, x, p)
    H[1,1] = 2 + 1200*x[1]^2 - 400*x[2]
    H[1,2] = H[2,1] = -400*x[1]
    H[2,2] = 200
    return H
end

optf_nr = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock, grad=rosenbrock_grad!, hess=rosenbrock_hess!)
prob_nr = OptimizationProblem(optf_nr, x0)
sol_nr = solve(prob_nr, Newton())

# BFGS
optf_bfgs = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock, grad=rosenbrock_grad!)
prob_bfgs = OptimizationProblem(optf_bfgs, x0)
sol_bfgs = solve(prob_bfgs, BFGS())

# L-BFGS
sol_lbfgs = solve(prob_bfgs, LBFGS())

println("Newton-Raphson: x* = ", sol_nr.u, ", iterations = ", sol_nr.stats.iterations)
println("BFGS: x* = ", sol_bfgs.u, ", iterations = ", sol_bfgs.stats.iterations) 
println("L-BFGS: x* = ", sol_lbfgs.u, ", iterations = ", sol_lbfgs.stats.iterations)

Theorem 8.3 (BFGS Superlinear Convergence). Under standard regularity conditions, BFGS converges superlinearly to the optimum, meaning ||θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ - θ||/||θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - θ|| → 0.

The proof involves showing that the BFGS approximation converges to the true inverse Hessian in a specific sense.

4.14 Coordinate Descent Methods

Coordinate descent optimizes one parameter at a time while holding others fixed. This approach proves especially effective for problems with separable structure or sparsity-inducing penalties.

4.14.1 LASSO via Coordinate Descent

The LASSO optimization problem is:

minimize_{β} ½||y - Xβ||² + λ||β||₁

The ℓ₁ penalty is not differentiable, but coordinate descent can handle this non-smoothness effectively.

Algorithm 8.2 (LASSO Coordinate Descent). For j = 1, …, p: 1. Compute partial residual: r⁽⁻ʲ⁾ = y - X⁽⁻ʲ⁾β⁽⁻ʲ⁾ 2. Update: βⱼ ← S(Xⱼᵀr⁽⁻ʲ⁾/||Xⱼ||², λ/||Xⱼ||²)

where S(z, λ) = sign(z)max(|z| - λ, 0) is the soft-thresholding operator.

using LinearAlgebra

function soft_threshold(x, λ)
    return sign(x) * max(abs(x) - λ, 0)
end

function lasso_coordinate_descent(X, y, λ; maxiter=1000, tol=1e-6)
    n, p = size(X)
    β = zeros(p)
    
    # Precompute column norms (assuming X is standardized)
    X_norms_sq = sum(X.^2, dims=1)[:]
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        β_old = copy(β)
        
        for j in 1:p
            # Compute partial residual
            r = y - X * β + X[:, j] * β[j]
            
            # Coordinate update with soft thresholding
            β[j] = soft_threshold(X[:, j]' * r / X_norms_sq[j], λ / X_norms_sq[j])
        end
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(β - β_old) < tol
            return β, iter
        end
    end
    
    return β, maxiter
end

# Example: LASSO regression
Random.seed!(42)
n, p = 100, 20
X = randn(n, p)
X = X ./ sqrt.(sum(X.^2, dims=1))  # Standardize columns
β_true = [2.0, -1.5, 1.0, zeros(p-3)...]  # Sparse true coefficients
y = X * β_true + 0.1 * randn(n)

# Fit LASSO for different λ values
λ_values = [0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0]
results = Dict()

for λ in λ_values
    β_hat, iters = lasso_coordinate_descent(X, y, λ)
    results[λ] = β_hat
    println("λ = $λ: iterations = $iters, ||β||₀ = ", count(x -> abs(x) > 1e-6, β_hat))
end

# Compare with true coefficients
println("True β (first 6): ", β_true[1:6])
for λ in λ_values
    println("λ = $λ (first 6): ", round.(results[λ][1:6], digits=3))
end

4.14.2 Elastic Net Coordinate Descent

The elastic net combines ℓ₁ and ℓ₂ penalties:

minimize_{β} ½||y - Xβ||² + λ₁||β||₁ + λ₂||β||₂²

The coordinate update becomes: βⱼ ← S(Xⱼᵀr⁽⁻ʲ⁾/(||Xⱼ||² + λ₂), λ₁/(||Xⱼ||² + λ₂))

This modification handles correlated predictors better than pure LASSO by encouraging grouped selection through the ℓ₂ penalty.

4.15 Proximal Gradient Methods

Many statistical optimization problems have the form:

minimize f(θ) + g(θ)

where f is smooth and g is convex but possibly non-smooth (like ℓ₁ penalties). Proximal gradient methods handle this structure elegantly.

Definition 8.2 (Proximal Operator). For a convex function g and parameter t > 0, the proximal operator is

prox_{tg}(x) = argmin_z {½||z - x||² + tg(z)}

The proximal gradient method alternates between gradient steps on f and proximal steps on g:

θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ = prox_{αₜg}(θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - αₜ∇f(θ⁽ᵗ⁾))

Example 8.2: Fused LASSO

The fused LASSO penalizes both sparsity and lack of smoothness:

minimize ½||y - Xβ||² + λ₁||β||₁ + λ₂||Dβ||₁

where D is the difference operator. The proximal operator for the fused penalty can be computed efficiently using specialized algorithms.

function fused_lasso_proximal(β, λ1, λ2, t)
    p = length(β)
    β_new = copy(β)
    
    # First apply ℓ₁ proximal operator (soft thresholding)
    β_new = soft_threshold.(β_new, t * λ1)
    
    # Then apply fused penalty proximal operator
    # (Simplified version - full implementation requires isotonic regression)
    for iter in 1:10  # Few iterations of approximate algorithm
        for i in 2:p-1
            # Local averaging with soft thresholding
            local_avg = (β_new[i-1] + β_new[i+1]) / 2
            β_new[i] = soft_threshold(0.5 * β_new[i] + 0.5 * local_avg, t * λ2 / 2)
        end
    end
    
    return β_new
end

# Proximal gradient method for fused LASSO
function fused_lasso_proximal_gradient(X, y, λ1, λ2; maxiter=1000, α=0.01)
    n, p = size(X)
    β = zeros(p)
    XtX = X' * X
    Xty = X' * y
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # Gradient step on smooth part
        grad_f = XtX * β - Xty
        β_grad = β - α * grad_f
        
        # Proximal step on non-smooth part
        β = fused_lasso_proximal(β_grad, λ1, λ2, α)
    end
    
    return β
end

4.15.1 Accelerated Proximal Gradient (FISTA)

The Fast Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (FISTA) accelerates proximal gradient methods using Nesterov momentum:

Algorithm 8.3 (FISTA). Initialize θ₀ = z₀, t₀ = 1: 1. θₜ₊₁ = prox_{αg}(zₜ - α∇f(zₜ)) 2. tₜ₊₁ = (1 + √(1 + 4tₖ²))/2 3. zₜ₊₁ = θₜ₊₁ + ((tₜ - 1)/tₜ₊₁)(θₜ₊₁ - θₜ)

FISTA achieves O(1/k²) convergence rate compared to O(1/k) for standard proximal gradient methods.

4.16 Stochastic Gradient Methods

For large datasets, computing the full gradient becomes computationally prohibitive. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) uses random subsets of data to approximate gradients, enabling scalable optimization.

4.16.1 Basic Stochastic Gradient Descent

Given objective f(θ) = (1/n)Σᵢfᵢ(θ), SGD approximates the gradient using a minibatch:

θ⁽ᵗ⁺¹⁾ = θ⁽ᵗ⁾ - αₜ(1/|Bₜ|)Σᵢ∈Bₜ ∇fᵢ(θ⁽ᵗ⁾)

where Bₜ is a randomly selected minibatch and αₜ is the learning rate.

4.16.2 Convergence Theory for SGD

Theorem 8.4 (SGD Convergence). Assume f is L-smooth and μ-strongly convex, and the stochastic gradients satisfy E[||∇̃f(θ) - ∇f(θ)||²] ≤ σ². If the learning rate satisfies αₜ = α/(1 + γt) with appropriate α and γ, then

E[f(θ̄ₜ) - f*] ≤ O(log(t)/t)

where θ̄ₜ is the average of iterates.

The proof relies on analyzing the bias-variance decomposition of the stochastic gradient estimator and showing that averaging reduces variance while maintaining convergence.

4.16.3 Learning Rate Schedules

The choice of learning rate schedule critically affects SGD performance:

  1. Constant: αₜ = α (simple but may not converge)
  2. Step decay: αₜ = α × γ^⌊t/s⌋ (reduces by factor γ every s steps)
  3. Exponential decay: αₜ = α₀e^(-λt)
  4. Inverse decay: αₜ = α/(1 + βt)
using Statistics

function sgd_logistic_regression(X, y, batch_size; 
                                maxiter=1000, lr=0.01, lr_schedule=:constant)
    n, p = size(X)
    β = randn(p) * 0.01  # Small random initialization
    losses = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # Sample minibatch
        batch_idx = sample(1:n, batch_size, replace=false)
        X_batch = X[batch_idx, :]
        y_batch = y[batch_idx]
        
        # Compute predictions and loss
        logits = X_batch * β
        probs = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logits))
        loss = -mean(y_batch .* logits - log.(1 .+ exp.(logits)))
        push!(losses, loss)
        
        # Compute gradient
        grad = X_batch' * (probs - y_batch) / batch_size
        
        # Update learning rate
        if lr_schedule == :step_decay && iter % 100 == 0
            lr *= 0.9
        elseif lr_schedule == :inverse_decay
            lr = lr / (1 + 0.001 * iter)
        end
        
        # SGD update
        β -= lr * grad
    end
    
    return β, losses
end

# Example: Compare SGD with different learning rate schedules
Random.seed!(123)
n, p = 5000, 10
X = [ones(n) randn(n, p-1)]
β_true = randn(p)
logits = X * β_true
y = rand.(Bernoulli.(1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logits))))

schedules = [:constant, :step_decay, :inverse_decay]
results = Dict()

for schedule in schedules
    β_sgd, losses = sgd_logistic_regression(X, y, 50, lr=0.1, lr_schedule=schedule)
    results[schedule] = (β_sgd, losses)
    println("Schedule: $schedule, Final loss: ", losses[end])
end

4.16.4 Adam Optimizer

The Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) optimizer combines ideas from SGD with momentum and adaptive learning rates.

Algorithm 8.4 (Adam Optimizer). Initialize m₀ = 0, v₀ = 0: 1. gₜ = ∇f(θₜ₋₁) (possibly stochastic) 2. mₜ = β₁mₜ₋₁ + (1-β₁)gₜ (first moment estimate) 3. vₜ = β₂vₜ₋₁ + (1-β₂)gₜ² (second moment estimate) 4. m̂ₜ = mₜ/(1-β₁ᵗ), v̂ₜ = vₜ/(1-β₂ᵗ) (bias correction) 5. θₜ = θₜ₋₁ - α·m̂ₜ/(√v̂ₜ + ε)

Typical parameters: β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.999, ε = 10⁻⁸, α = 0.001.

mutable struct AdamOptimizer
    lr::Float64
    β1::Float64
    β2::Float64
    ε::Float64
    m::Vector{Float64}
    v::Vector{Float64}
    t::Int
end

function AdamOptimizer(dim; lr=0.001, β1=0.9, β2=0.999, ε=1e-8)
    return AdamOptimizer(lr, β1, β2, ε, zeros(dim), zeros(dim), 0)
end

function update!(opt::AdamOptimizer, θ, grad)
    opt.t += 1
    
    # Update biased moment estimates
    opt.m .= opt.β1 .* opt.m .+ (1 - opt.β1) .* grad
    opt.v .= opt.β2 .* opt.v .+ (1 - opt.β2) .* (grad .^ 2)
    
    # Bias correction
    m_hat = opt.m ./ (1 - opt.β1^opt.t)
    v_hat = opt.v ./ (1 - opt.β2^opt.t)
    
    # Parameter update
    θ .-= opt.lr .* m_hat ./ (sqrt.(v_hat) .+ opt.ε)
    
    return θ
end

# Compare Adam with SGD
function compare_optimizers(X, y, batch_size; maxiter=500)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # SGD
    β_sgd = randn(p) * 0.01
    losses_sgd = Float64[]
    
    # Adam
    β_adam = copy(β_sgd)
    adam_opt = AdamOptimizer(p)
    losses_adam = Float64[]
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # Sample minibatch
        batch_idx = sample(1:n, batch_size, replace=false)
        X_batch = X[batch_idx, :]
        y_batch = y[batch_idx]
        
        # Compute loss and gradient for both
        logits_sgd = X_batch * β_sgd
        logits_adam = X_batch * β_adam
        
        probs_sgd = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logits_sgd))
        probs_adam = 1 ./ (1 .+ exp.(-logits_adam))
        
        loss_sgd = -mean(y_batch .* logits_sgd - log.(1 .+ exp.(logits_sgd)))
        loss_adam = -mean(y_batch .* logits_adam - log.(1 .+ exp.(logits_adam)))
        
        push!(losses_sgd, loss_sgd)
        push!(losses_adam, loss_adam)
        
        grad_sgd = X_batch' * (probs_sgd - y_batch) / batch_size
        grad_adam = X_batch' * (probs_adam - y_batch) / batch_size
        
        # Updates
        β_sgd .-= 0.01 * grad_sgd  # Fixed learning rate SGD
        β_adam = update!(adam_opt, β_adam, grad_adam)
    end
    
    return (β_sgd, losses_sgd), (β_adam, losses_adam)
end

sgd_result, adam_result = compare_optimizers(X, y, 64)
println("Final SGD loss: ", sgd_result[2][end])
println("Final Adam loss: ", adam_result[2][end])

4.17 Natural Gradient Descent

Natural gradient descent incorporates the geometry of the parameter space through the Fisher information metric, connecting optimization to information geometry.

Definition 8.3 (Natural Gradient). For a parametric family {p(x|θ)}, the natural gradient is

∇̃f(θ) = I⁻¹(θ)∇f(θ)

where I(θ) is the Fisher information matrix.

The natural gradient provides the steepest ascent direction in the space of probability distributions when measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence.

Theorem 8.5 (Natural Gradient Interpretation). The natural gradient direction minimizes the KL divergence between p(·|θ) and p(·|θ + δθ) subject to a constraint on the expected change in the objective function.

Proof sketch. The constraint optimization problem leads to the Lagrangian involving the Fisher information metric, yielding the natural gradient as the optimal direction. □

Example 8.3: Natural Gradient for Gaussian Mixture Models

For a Gaussian mixture model, the Fisher information matrix blocks according to the parameter structure (mixing weights, means, covariances). Natural gradient updates respect this geometry, often leading to faster convergence than standard gradient methods.

using Distributions

function natural_gradient_gmm(data, K; maxiter=100, lr=0.01)
    n, d = size(data)
    
    # Initialize parameters
    π = ones(K) / K  # Mixing weights
    μ = [randn(d) for _ in 1:K]  # Means
    Σ = [diagm(ones(d)) for _ in 1:K]  # Covariances (diagonal)
    
    for iter in 1:maxiter
        # E-step: compute responsibilities
        γ = zeros(n, K)
        for i in 1:n, k in 1:K
            γ[i, k] = π[k] * pdf(MvNormal(μ[k], Σ[k]), data[i, :])
        end
        γ ./= sum(γ, dims=2)  # Normalize
        
        # Compute Fisher information blocks (simplified)
        N_k = sum(γ, dims=1)[:]
        
        # Natural gradient updates
        # Mixing weights (using Dirichlet Fisher metric)
        π_grad = N_k - n * π
        π += lr * π_grad ./ (n * π)  # Natural gradient with Dirichlet metric
        π ./= sum(π)  # Normalize
        
        # Means (weighted by precision)
        for k in 1:K
            if N_k[k] > 0
                μ_new = sum(γ[:, k] .* data, dims=1)[:] / N_k[k]
                # Natural gradient is just the standard gradient for means
                μ[k] = μ_new
            end
        end
        
        # Update covariances (simplified diagonal case)
        for k in 1:K
            if N_k[k] > 0
                diff = data .- μ[k]'
                Σ[k] = diagm(sum(γ[:, k] .* (diff .^ 2), dims=1)[:] / N_k[k])
            end
        end
    end
    
    return π, μ, Σ
end

4.18 Expectation Propagation
